attributes.stem:
A 26-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of anxiety symptoms. She is currently 26 weeks pregnant with her first child. She reports difficulty falling asleep due to racing thoughts about daily responsibilities, poor concentration at work, and constant tension. She describes feeling overwhelmed about becoming a parent. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. Her antenatal care is up to date with normal findings.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Mental state examination reveals anxious affect and thought content focused on worry about the future, but no features of depression or thoughts of self-harm.

After discussion of the risks and benefits of psychotherapy and medication management, she expresses a preference for medication while awaiting psychology review.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate medication to commence?
attributes.explanation:
This patient describes excessive anxiety and worries, occurring for at least 6 months, about a number of events or activities that causes them significant distress. Their anxiety and worry are associated with sleep disturbance, restlessness, difficulty concentrating, and muscle tension. This is suggestive of generalised anxiety disorder. Treatment for generalised anxiety disorder includes psychoeducational, psychological therapies (e.g. CBT), and pharmacotherapy.

SSRIs (citalopram, escitalopram, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, paroxetine, sertraline) are first-line drugs for the treatment of generalised anxiety disorder. Moreover, SSRIs have the largest amount of pregnancy safety data of all antidepressants.

Sertraline is most commonly used because it has the most safety data. It is also appropriate to use during breastfeeding due to low levels of exposure in breastfeeding infants. The use of sertraline now will avoid the need for switching agents after pregnancy if the patient decides to breastfeed. Whilst sertraline can potentially cause sleep disturbances, this would still be first-line medication for a pregnant patient, and could be re-evaluated if sleep issues escalated after commencing the medication.

Paroxetine is associated with cardiac malformations and miscarriage.

Venlafaxine also has a risk of miscarriage that is similar to that of paroxetine.

A short-term benzodiazepine may be needed adjunctively with an antidepressant for maintenance therapy because it can take several weeks before an effect is seen with an antidepressant. However, its use in the third trimester can cause poor neonatal adaption, neonatal drowsiness, respiratory depression, poor temperature regulation, poor feeding and hypotonicity ('floppy infant syndrome'). This is especially true for agents with a long half-life (e.g. diazepam). Therefore, if required, an agent with a shorter half-life (eg temazepam, lorazepam) will be more appropriate.

Propranolol does not relieve anxiety itself. However, it may reduce distressing physiological symptoms associated with anxiety (tremor, palpitations and sweating). Therefore, it may play a role in the management of performance anxiety, not generalised anxiety disorder.

NOTE: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the neonate (PPHN) is a rare condition affecting 1 to 2 infants per 1000 live births. It has a high rate of mortality (5%) and severe morbidity (60%). Although fetal exposure to an SSRI after 20 weeks gestation is associated with a small increase in absolute risk of this syndrome, the risk is still extremely low and does not preclude the use of SSRIs in pregnancy.
learningPoints: Sertraline is the most commonly used SSRI in pregnancy and breastfeeding.
Generalised anxiety disorder in pregnancy requires careful consideration of medication safety profiles.
Psychological therapy remains an important component of anxiety management in pregnancy.
references: Lebin LG, Novick AM. Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs) in Pregnancy: An Updated Review on Risks to Mother, Fetus, and Child. Curr Psychiatry Rep. 2022;24(11):687-695. doi:10.1007/s11920-022-01372-x

McAllister-Williams RH, Baldwin DS, Cantwell R, Easter A, Gilvarry E, Glover V, Green L, Gregoire A, Howard LM, Jones I, Khalifeh H, Lingford-Hughes A, McDonald E, Micali N, Pariante CM, Peters L, Roberts A, Smith NC, Taylor D, Wieck A, Yates LM, Young AH; endorsed by the British Association for Psychopharmacology. British Association for Psychopharmacology consensus guidance on the use of psychotropic medication preconception, in pregnancy and postpartum 2017. J Psychopharmacol. 2017 May;31(5):519-552. doi: 10.1177/0269881117699361.

Womersley K, Ripullone K, Agius M. What are the risks associated with different Selective Serotonin Re-uptake Inhibitors (SSRIs) to treat depression and anxiety in pregnancy? An evaluation of current evidence. Psychiatr Danub. 2017 Sep;29(Suppl 3):629-644.
